one_liner: A 68-year-old male with hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with sudden-onset right-sided weakness and slurred speech for the past 90 minutes.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient's wife called 911 after she witnessed her husband suddenly slump in his chair while watching television approximately 90 minutes ago. He developed right-sided facial droop, right arm and leg weakness, and began speaking with slurred, incomprehensible speech. Symptoms have not improved since onset. The patient was in his usual state of health earlier today. His wife confirms he was last known well at 6:00 PM (it is now 7:30 PM). He denies (via head nod) headache, but cannot reliably communicate. No recent head trauma, seizure activity, or fever. He did not take his morning medications today.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension, atrial fibrillation (NOT on anticoagulation due to prior fall risk assessment), type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia
stage2.surgical_history: Right total hip replacement 3 years ago
stage2.medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily
stage2.family_history: Mother had a stroke at age 74. Father had coronary artery disease.
stage2.social_history: Retired accountant. Former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 10 years ago). Social alcohol, 1-2 drinks per week. Lives with wife. Independent at baseline in all ADLs.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Elderly male, awake, appears frustrated, unable to communicate clearly. HEENT: Pupils equal and reactive, 3mm bilaterally. Neck: Supple. Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm; S1/S2 present; No murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; Neuro (NIH this condition Scale = 14): Mental status: Alert, eyes open spontaneously; Global aphasia (cannot name, repeat, or follow complex commands); Able to follow simple one-step commands inconsistently; Cranial Nerves: Right lower facial droop; Gaze preference to the left; Right homonymous hemianopia; Motor: Right upper extremity 1/5 (drifts immediately), right lower extremity 2/5; Left side 5/5; Sensory: Diminished sensation to light touch on right side; Coordination: Unable to assess on right; No dysmetria on left; Gait: Unable to stand.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; must check glucose before tPA (hypoglycemia can mimic stroke)
Not anticoagulated; INR <1.7 allows tPA administration
Suboptimally controlled diabetes, contributes to stroke risk

imaging_results.result: No acute hemorrhage. No midline shift. No mass lesion. Early hypodense area in the left middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory. Hyperdense left MCA sign present.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Excludes hemorrhagic stroke; early ischemic changes and hyperdense MCA sign suggest acute left MCA occlusion

imaging_results.result: Complete occlusion of the left middle cerebral artery M1 segment. Patent carotid arteries bilaterally. Circle of Willis otherwise intact.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Large vessel occlusion (LVO) of left MCA confirmed; patient is a candidate for mechanical thrombectomy

imaging_results.result: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular rate (92 bpm). No acute ST changes.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: AFib is the likely embolic source of stroke

stage5.final_clue: Sudden-onset left MCA syndrome (right hemiparesis, global aphasia, right gaze preference) within the tPA window, with CT showing no hemorrhage and CTA confirming left MCA M1 occlusion.

debrief.classic_presentation: Sudden onset of focal neurological deficits corresponding to a vascular territory. Left MCA strokes present with contralateral (right) hemiparesis and hemisensory loss, right homonymous hemianopia, and aphasia (if dominant hemisphere). Key is the abrupt onset and identifiable time of last known well. Atrial fibrillation is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke.
debrief.pathophysiology: Ischemic stroke results from occlusion of a cerebral artery by thrombus or embolus, causing ischemia in the supplied territory. In cardioembolic stroke (as with AFib), a thrombus forms in the left atrial appendage and embolizes to cerebral arteries. The ischemic penumbra (tissue at risk but not yet infarcted) is the target of reperfusion therapy. Without reperfusion, ischemia progresses to irreversible infarction over hours. The MCA supplies the lateral frontal, parietal, and temporal lobes, making it the most common site of stroke.
debrief.treatment_overview: IV alteplase (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% bolus then remainder over 60 minutes, if within 4.5 hours of last known well and no contraindications. For large vessel occlusions (LVO), mechanical thrombectomy can be performed up to 24 hours with appropriate imaging selection (CT perfusion or MRI). Admit to stroke unit/ICU. Blood pressure management: permit up to 185/110 before tPA, then maintain <180/105 for 24 hours post-tPA. Initiate anticoagulation for AFib (usually after 24 hours post-tPA, pending repeat imaging). Long-term: anticoagulation (DOACs preferred), statin, control of risk factors.
high_yield_pearls: The only lab required BEFORE tPA administration is glucose; do not delay tPA for other lab results unless clinical suspicion for coagulopathy.
The hyperdense MCA sign on non-contrast CT indicates acute thrombus within the vessel.
Mechanical thrombectomy has extended the treatment window to 24 hours for select LVO patients based on perfusion imaging.
NIHSS score helps quantify deficit severity: >6 suggests LVO and possible thrombectomy candidacy.
AFib-related stroke has the highest recurrence rate without anticoagulation (~12% per year).
sources: 2019 AHA/ASA Guidelines for Early Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke (Stroke 2019;50:e344-e418) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=2019+AHA%2FASA+Guidelines+for+Early+Management+of+Acute+Ischemic+Stroke+%28Stroke+2019%3B50%3Ae344-e418%29

Goyal M et al. Lancet 2016;387:1723-1731 (HERMES meta-analysis on thrombectomy) — https://www.thelancet.com/action/doSearch?text1=Goyal+M+et+al.+Lancet+2016%3B387%3A1723-1731+%28HERMES+meta-analysis+on+thrombectomy%29
